summary: DeVault condemns Mozilla for secretly sideloading a Mr. Robot promotional browser extension into Firefox users' browsers through the 'experiments' feature. He traces a pattern of increasingly invasive monetization decisions, from Google as default search to Pocket integration to this advertisement disguised as an experiment. The extension modified webpage text and added HTTP headers, and was intended to be silently installed without user knowledge. DeVault argues this fundamentally violates user trust and the core purpose of a web browser as a faithful tool. He announces he will stop using and recommending Firefox entirely. Updates note Mozilla eventually rolled back the study and issued an apology, but DeVault stands by his criticism.
key_points: Mozilla sideloaded a promotional browser extension for NBC's Mr. Robot into Firefox stable via the 'experiments' feature, which is enabled by default and has been known to re-enable itself
The extension modified webpage content and added HTTP headers, violating the browser's fundamental duty to display websites faithfully
Mozilla's pattern of monetization decisions — Google defaults, Pocket bundling, new tab paid links, and now sideloaded ads — represents an escalating erosion of user trust
Bug tracker discussions were locked down and critical user comments were removed, compounding the transparency problem
DeVault declares the incident a terminal breach of trust, switching permanently to qutebrowser and ceasing all Firefox recommendations
money_quotes: Whoever is leading these decisions at Mozilla needs to be stopped.
If I saw this before I knew what was going on, I would think my browser was compromised!
First of all, web browsers are a tool. I don't want my browser to fool around, I just want it to display websites faithfully.
When I compile vim with gcc, I don't want gcc to make vim sporadically add "fsociety" into every document I write. I want it to compile vim and go away.
This event tells us that "Firefox studies" into a backdoor for advertisements, and I will never trust it again.
There is no amount of money that you should have accepted for this.
This is the last straw - and I took a lot of straws from you. Goodbye forever, Mozilla.
Mozilla, you fucked up bad, and you still haven't apologised.
key_insight: When a browser meant to serve users becomes a vector for silent advertising, it has betrayed its fundamental purpose and forfeited user trust permanently.
